blocks.html_en:
> "superficially superior code"

Can you unpack that a bit? It produces measurably, meaningfully inferior code everywhere I see it in use.

> "leadership encourages this because from what they can tell, there is no downside"

As said 'leadership' I find this a bit puzzling. I'm seeing strong, quantifiable evidence of increasing churn, increasing incident count, and length of downtime from the date of our biggest push into GenAI, and I'm organizing efforts on my teams to mitigate those issues and actively reduce GenAI adoption.

If you mean my c-suite, you're mostly correct although they are already rumbling about seeing zero or negative ROI on GenAI investments.

> Anyone not using LLMs all day is just not going to be as prolific

Agreed, but prolific != productive.

sentences.en: > "superficially superior code"Can you unpack that a bit?
sentences.zh: >“表面上优越的代码”你能解压一下吗？

sentences.en: It produces measurably, meaningfully inferior code everywhere I see it in use.> "leadership encourages this because from what they can tell, there is no downside"As said 'leadership' I find this a bit puzzling.
sentences.zh: 在我看到它使用的任何地方，它都会产生明显的、有意义的劣质代码。>“领导层鼓励这样做，因为从他们所知，没有任何缺点”正如“领导层”所说，我发现这有点令人费解。

sentences.en: I'm seeing strong, quantifiable evidence of increasing churn, increasing incident count, and length of downtime from the date of our biggest push into GenAI, and I'm organizing efforts on my teams to mitigate those issues and actively reduce GenAI adoption.If you mean my c-suite, you're mostly correct although they are already rumbling about seeing zero or negative ROI on GenAI investments.> Anyone not using LLMs all day is just not going to be as prolificAgreed, but prolific != productive.
sentences.zh: 我看到了强有力的、可量化的证据，表明从我们最大程度地推进 GenAI 之日起，客户流失率不断增加，事件数量不断增加，停机时间也不断增加，我正在组织我的团队努力缓解这些问题并积极减少 GenAI 的采用。如果你指的是我的高管，那么你基本上是正确的，尽管他们已经在抱怨 GenAI 投资的投资回报率为零或负数。> 任何整天不使用法学硕士的人都不会像我们所同意的那样多产，但会多产!= 富有成效。

blocks.html_en:
Sure, by "superficially superior code" I mean that LLMs, by design, produce code that has syntax and idioms that are perfectly in line with industry norms due to the vast training corpus that it comes from. It looks great at first glance.

Normally, this is a signal that the developer is at least informed about best practices in the language and is usually a pretty good indicator that they're thinking clearly about the work they're doing. That, along with reputation, takes a lot of cognitive burden off the reviewer.

However, with LLMs that is all it means. You can be sure the code is norm-compliant, but in my experience that is all you can be sure of. There's not really a guarantee that it has thought deeply about the problem space.

It's like a bridge that looks great.

sentences.en: Sure, by "superficially superior code" I mean that LLMs, by design, produce code that has syntax and idioms that are perfectly in line with industry norms due to the vast training corpus that it comes from.
sentences.zh: 当然，我所说的“表面上优越的代码”是指法学硕士在设计上所生成的代码的语法和习惯用法完全符合行业规范，因为它来自庞大的培训语料库。

sentences.en: It looks great at first glance.Normally, this is a signal that the developer is at least informed about best practices in the language and is usually a pretty good indicator that they're thinking clearly about the work they're doing.
sentences.zh: 乍一看它看起来很棒。通常，这是一个信号，表明开发人员至少了解该语言的最佳实践，并且通常是一个很好的指标，表明他们正在清楚地思考他们正在做的工作。

sentences.en: That, along with reputation, takes a lot of cognitive burden off the reviewer.However, with LLMs that is all it means.
sentences.zh: 这与声誉一起，减轻了审稿人的很多认知负担。然而，对于法学硕士来说，这就是它的全部意义。

sentences.en: You can be sure the code is norm-compliant, but in my experience that is all you can be sure of.
sentences.zh: 您可以确定代码符合规范，但根据我的经验，您只能确定这一点。

sentences.en: There's not really a guarantee that it has thought deeply about the problem space.It's like a bridge that looks great.
sentences.zh: 并不能真正保证它已经深入思考了问题空间。它就像一座看起来很棒的桥梁。

blocks.html_en:
> leadership

Not sure if its just me, lately I have started feeling pretty offensive about the increased usage of the word. Its management not leadership by any means.

> "leadership encourages this because from what they can tell, there is no downside"

For most people in management its easier to pick the current set of slangs/abbreviation's, general trend and go with it. Understanding the details would take time, raise questions and no one in management has time or political capital to spend on it.

sentences.en: > leadershipNot sure if its just me, lately I have started feeling pretty offensive about the increased usage of the word.
sentences.zh: > 领导力 不确定是不是只有我这么认为，最近我开始对这个词的使用增加感到非常反感。

sentences.en: Its management not leadership by any means.> "leadership encourages this because from what they can tell, there is no downside"For most people in management its easier to pick the current set of slangs/abbreviation's, general trend and go with it.
sentences.zh: 无论如何，它是管理而不是领导。>“领导鼓励这样做，因为从他们看来，没有什么坏处”对于大多数管理人员来说，选择当前的俚语/缩写、总体趋势并遵循它会更容易。

sentences.en: Understanding the details would take time, raise questions and no one in management has time or political capital to spend on it.
sentences.zh: 了解细节需要时间，会引发问题，而且管理层中没有人有时间或政治资本花在这上面。

blocks.html_en:
The code written by agents generally seems to reflect what you (the human driver) asked for. If you discuss the approach and architecture first, asking the right questions in the process, and then let it implement - the result is quite on point with the frontier models. Might need some minor touch-ups if agent missed some common conventions or guessed the expectations wrong (but again, salvageable with follow-up prompts). And when it comes to line-by-line logic within functions, I would argue that today's models are LESS likely to make a mistake in there than humans - especially if you cross-review with another model (e.g. "write with Claude, review with GPT").

Humans write slop too, you know. Just saying.

sentences.en: The code written by agents generally seems to reflect what you (the human driver) asked for.
sentences.zh: 代理编写的代码通常似乎反映了您（人类驾驶员）的要求。

sentences.en: If you discuss the approach and architecture first, asking the right questions in the process, and then let it implement - the result is quite on point with the frontier models.
sentences.zh: 如果您首先讨论方法和架构，在过程中提出正确的问题，然后让它实施 - 结果与前沿模型非常吻合。

sentences.en: Might need some minor touch-ups if agent missed some common conventions or guessed the expectations wrong (but again, salvageable with follow-up prompts).
sentences.zh: 如果代理错过了一些常见约定或猜错了期望，可能需要一些小的修改（但同样，可以通过后续提示进行挽救）。

sentences.en: And when it comes to line-by-line logic within functions, I would argue that today's models are LESS likely to make a mistake in there than humans - especially if you cross-review with another model (e.g. "write with Claude, review with GPT").Humans write slop too, you know.
sentences.zh: 当谈到函数内的逐行逻辑时，我认为今天的模型比人类更不可能在其中犯错误 - 特别是如果你与另一个模型进行交叉审查（例如“与 Claude 一起编写，用 GPT 进行审查”）。你知道，人类也会写潦草的东西。

sentences.en: Just saying.
sentences.zh: 只是说。

blocks.html_en:
> Leadership encourages this because from what they can tell, there is no downside.

This was my big fear before we saw price increases. Now I'm pinning all my hopes on AI being too expensive to justify further big corporate pushes. (Sigh.) I love having new tools, but I hate being pushed to use ______ tool to meet some managerial metric.

blocks.html_en:
As with all text-only AI detection schemes, I am concerned about the accuracy of the detection. I'm skeptical of the methodology, specifically the final join of the three detector scores---how can you be sure that that final step does not introduce any biases? There's no source available, so it's difficult to tell exactly how this works or reproduce the research.

I've also uploaded text samples from my own (unreleased) research from pre-LLM era, and it's seemingly scoring pretty high on the LLM-detection scores. On other papers, nearly every sentence is highlighted as red "machine-leaning," but that does not impact the score? Additionally, there are dramatic differences between the scores for identical text with and without LaTeX formatting, despite the fact that it should not matter.

The takeaway from this should be "it is difficult to detect generated text and we should be careful about accepting results simply because they confirm a hypothesis."

--

Relatedly, the text above scores as highly machine-written, despite the fact that I just wrote it with my human hands, I promise :)

sentences.zh: 我对这种方法持怀疑态度，特别是三个检测器分数的最终连接——你如何确定最后一步不会引入任何偏差？

sentences.zh: 在其他论文中，几乎每个句子都突出显示为红色“机器学习”，但这不会影响分数吗？

sentences.en: Additionally, there are dramatic differences between the scores for identical text with and without LaTeX formatting, despite the fact that it should not matter.The takeaway from this should be "it is difficult to detect generated text and we should be careful about accepting results simply because they confirm a hypothesis."--Relatedly, the text above scores as highly machine-written, despite the fact that I just wrote it with my human hands, I promise :)

blocks.html_en:
> If a tool marks 40% of new papers as machine-written but also marks 20% of papers written before ChatGPT existed, the real story is the 20% nobody mentioned.

When 65% of the papers you read have the characteristics of being AI written, whether or not you use AI to write, your writing will be influenced by the AI style. I imagine this must be particularly the case for newbie researchers who are still developing their writing style

sentences.en: > If a tool marks 40% of new papers as machine-written but also marks 20% of papers written before ChatGPT existed, the real story is the 20% nobody mentioned.When 65% of the papers you read have the characteristics of being AI written, whether or not you use AI to write, your writing will be influenced by the AI style.
sentences.zh: > 如果一个工具将 40% 的新论文标记为机器写的，同时也将 20% 的 ChatGPT 之前写的论文标记为机器写的，那么真正的故事是那 20% 没有人提到的。当你阅读的论文中有 65% 具有 AI 写的特征时，无论你是否使用 AI 写，你的写作都会受到 AI 风格的影响。

sentences.en: I imagine this must be particularly the case for newbie researchers who are still developing their writing style
sentences.zh: 我想对于仍在发展写作风格的新手研究人员来说尤其如此

blocks.html_en:
even if your writing is influenced by "the AI style," it would likely only show up in patches, a word or rhetorical flourish maybe, but AI-generated text doesn't "sound like AI" in patches, it does so consistently across the entirety of the output. it's very hard for a person to maintain the syntactical patterns of AI writing over an extended period of time unless they know exactly what those are -- probably more knowledge than any person currently has, really -- and are applying a level of detail to each clause comparable to forging a painting.

there's also the fact that "the AI style" has changed over time. for instance, the word "delve" is notorious as an "AI sign," which it was up until mid-2024, at which point it dropped off sharply and has now basically disappeared from LLM output. so if someone happened to pick that up due to reading it everywhere, their writing is now less characteristic of AI, not more.

sentences.en: even if your writing is influenced by "the AI style," it would likely only show up in patches, a word or rhetorical flourish maybe, but AI-generated text doesn't "sound like AI" in patches, it does so consistently across the entirety of the output.
sentences.zh: 即使你的写作受到“人工智能风格”的影响，它也可能只会以补丁的形式出现，也许是一个单词或华丽的修辞，但人工智能生成的文本并不“听起来像人工智能”的补丁，它在整个输出中始终如一。

sentences.en: it's very hard for a person to maintain the syntactical patterns of AI writing over an extended period of time unless they know exactly what those are -- probably more knowledge than any person currently has, really -- and are applying a level of detail to each clause comparable to forging a painting.there's also the fact that "the AI style" has changed over time.
sentences.zh: 对于一个人来说，在很长一段时间内保持人工智能写作的句法模式是非常困难的，除非他们确切地知道这些是什么——实际上可能比任何人目前拥有的知识都多——并且对每个条款应用一定程度的细节，就像伪造一幅画一样。还有一个事实是，“人工智能风格”随着时间的推移而发生了变化。

sentences.en: for instance, the word "delve" is notorious as an "AI sign," which it was up until mid-2024, at which point it dropped off sharply and has now basically disappeared from LLM output.
sentences.zh: 例如，“delve”这个词作为“AI标志”而臭名昭著，直到2024年中期它才急剧下降，现在基本上已经从LLM输出中消失了。

sentences.en: so if someone happened to pick that up due to reading it everywhere, their writing is now less characteristic of AI, not more.
sentences.zh: 因此，如果有人因为到处阅读而碰巧发现了它，那么他们的写作现在就不再具有人工智能的特征，而不是更多。

blocks.html_en:
I might be missing something but what’s the real story of the 20%.

To me it sounds like 1. Either your tool is just not that good and reliable as you thought, 2. AI is trained on human written articles, so some of that human written content informed the now established “AI slop”.

There are people who shipped “slop” before AI.

sentences.zh: 我可能遗漏了一些东西，但 20% 的真实故事是什么。对我来说，这听起来像是 1。

blocks.html_en:
> There are people who shipped “slop” before AI.

The funny thing is that "slop" was defined by the writing habits of AI model, which we have learned to pick upon and recognize.

The "It's not X, it's Y", the rhetorical questions and other patterns would have been the tools of a skilled writer, and those people writing "like AI" before AI most likely would have been recognized as such.

sentences.en: > There are people who shipped “slop” before AI.The funny thing is that "slop" was defined by the writing habits of AI model, which we have learned to pick upon and recognize.The "It's not X, it's Y", the rhetorical questions and other patterns would have been the tools of a skilled writer, and those people writing "like AI" before AI most likely would have been recognized as such.
sentences.zh: > 有些人在人工智能之前就发表了“slop”。有趣的是，“slop”是由AI模型的写作习惯定义的，我们已经学会了挑选和识别。“这不是X，这是Y”，反问句和其他模式可能是熟练作家的工具，而那些在AI之前写“像AI”的人很可能会被认为是这样。

blocks.html_en:
I've looked at this same problem in an academic environment and have come to conclude that there is no way to reliably detect AI writing using only text. The reason for this is that no detector can take two identical inputs and classify one as synthetic (LLM) and one as organic (Human) and this situation can easily happen at the sentence or even paragraph level. Posed as a question, if a human writes a paragraph that happens to be exactly the same as a paragraph written by an LLM how do you classify that paragraph? There are a finite number of words and a finite way of combining them within an academic setting/field which means that we can't build a perfect classifier with just text. In practice, there are obvious tells and LLM-isms but these also change with time and each model has different tells so that even if a paper is full of LLM like writing we have no way to disambiguate between organic text that appears synthetic or synthetic text that appears organic.

A more interesting question, to me, is looking at a corpus of essays and analyzing how writing has changed with the introduction of LLMs. We can look at changes in vocabulary, linguistic features, style embeddings, regular embeddings, typos, errors, and references over time. When looked at in this way it is clear that academic writing has changed at the population level but what has led the change is harder to track down.

sentences.en: I've looked at this same problem in an academic environment and have come to conclude that there is no way to reliably detect AI writing using only text.
sentences.zh: 我在学术环境中研究过同样的问题，并得出结论：没有办法仅使用文本来可靠地检测人工智能写作。

sentences.en: The reason for this is that no detector can take two identical inputs and classify one as synthetic (LLM) and one as organic (Human) and this situation can easily happen at the sentence or even paragraph level.
sentences.zh: 原因是没有检测器可以接受两个相同的输入并将一个分类为合成（LLM），一个分类为有机（人类），并且这种情况很容易在句子甚至段落级别发生。

sentences.en: Posed as a question, if a human writes a paragraph that happens to be exactly the same as a paragraph written by an LLM how do you classify that paragraph?
sentences.zh: 提出一个问题，如果一个人写的一个段落恰好与法学硕士写的一个段落完全相同，您如何对该段落进行分类？

sentences.en: There are a finite number of words and a finite way of combining them within an academic setting/field which means that we can't build a perfect classifier with just text.
sentences.zh: 在学术环境/领域内，单词的数量和组合它们的方式是有限的，这意味着我们无法仅用文本构建完美的分类器。

sentences.en: In practice, there are obvious tells and LLM-isms but these also change with time and each model has different tells so that even if a paper is full of LLM like writing we have no way to disambiguate between organic text that appears synthetic or synthetic text that appears organic.A more interesting question, to me, is looking at a corpus of essays and analyzing how writing has changed with the introduction of LLMs.
sentences.zh: 在实践中，有明显的叙述和法学硕士主义，但这些也会随着时间而变化，每个模型都有不同的叙述，因此即使一篇论文充满了类似写作的法学硕士，我们也无法消除看似合成的有机文本或看似有机的合成文本之间的歧义。对我来说，一个更有趣的问题是查看论文语料库并分析随着法学硕士的引入，写作如何发生变化。

sentences.en: We can look at changes in vocabulary, linguistic features, style embeddings, regular embeddings, typos, errors, and references over time.
sentences.zh: 我们可以查看词汇、语言特征、风格嵌入、常规嵌入、拼写错误、错误和引用随时间的变化。

sentences.en: When looked at in this way it is clear that academic writing has changed at the population level but what has led the change is harder to track down.
sentences.zh: 从这个角度来看，很明显学术写作在人口层面发生了变化，但导致这种变化的原因却很难追踪。

sentences.zh: 对我来说，一个更有趣的问题是查看论文集并分析随着法学硕士的引入，写作发生了怎样的变化。

sentences.en: We can look at changes in vocabulary, linguistic features, style embeddings, regular embeddings, typos, errors, and references over time.
sentences.zh: 我们可以查看词汇、语言特征、风格嵌入、常规嵌入、拼写错误、错误和引用随时间的变化。

sentences.en: When looked at in this way it is clear that academic writing has changed at the population level but what has led the change is harder to track down.
sentences.zh: 从这个角度来看，很明显学术写作在人口层面发生了变化，但导致这种变化的原因却很难追踪。

blocks.html_en: When some closed models are "retired", interestingly we might not even be able to extract all their tells. We'd be limited to people writing about them on forums (where they don't often specify version, and there's a lot of memes and confirmation bias) and I don't know, doing some statistical Bayesian guessing that given the likelihood it's FooGPT 10.5, maybe we should update our beliefs about its characteristics etc.

sentences.en: When some closed models are "retired", interestingly we might not even be able to extract all their tells.
sentences.zh: 有趣的是，当一些封闭模型“退休”时，我们甚至可能无法提取它们的所有信息。

blocks.html_en:
> There are a finite number of words and a finite way of combining them within an academic setting/field which means that we can't build a perfect classifier with just text.

Maybe for very short phrases, but otherwise I disagree. Phrasing very quickly runs into a combinatorial explosion. In the words of Noam Chomsky, "Virtually every sentence that a person utters or understands is a brand-new combination of words, appearing for the first time in the history of the universe."

In my opinion, the difficulty in LLM/human text discrimination isn't that a person might coincidentally write exactly the same text as an LLM would, but rather that 1) LLMs aren't hard locked to a single phrasing (so this is a tougher problem than matching to a single static document, e.g. plagiarism detection) and 2) text has relatively low information density, so you need quite a bit of it to gather enough data to run a statistical test with a reasonably narrow confidence interval.

sentences.en: > There are a finite number of words and a finite way of combining them within an academic setting/field which means that we can't build a perfect classifier with just text.Maybe for very short phrases, but otherwise I disagree.
sentences.zh: > 在学术环境/领域内，单词数量和组合它们的方式是有限的，这意味着我们无法仅用文本构建完美的分类器。也许对于非常短的短语，但除此之外我不同意。

sentences.en: Phrasing very quickly runs into a combinatorial explosion.
sentences.zh: 措辞很快就会变成组合爆炸。

blocks.html_en:
I am not a native English speaker. This is not surprising to me. I think most of the papers we write would be flagged by AI detectors.

It is not because we ask LLM to write us a paper about X. It is because we are bad at writing in a scientific style, and american editors expect us to do it. With LLMs, we can write in basic sentences and tell the LLM the idea and it converts that to nice writing.

If you write each paragraph and have an LLM make that paragraph more scientific, it is entirely your paper, but it is flagged as LLM generated. If you have an LLM write the paper but speak good enough English, you can make it look human even though it is not human.

sentences.en: I am not a native English speaker.
sentences.zh: 我的母语不是英语。

sentences.en: This is not surprising to me.
sentences.zh: 这对我来说并不奇怪。

sentences.en: I think most of the papers we write would be flagged by AI detectors.It is not because we ask LLM to write us a paper about X.
sentences.zh: 我认为我们写的大部分论文都会被 AI 检测器标记。这并不是因为我们要求 LLM 给我们写一篇关于 X 的论文。

sentences.en: It is because we are bad at writing in a scientific style, and american editors expect us to do it.
sentences.zh: 这是因为我们不擅长以科学的方式写作，而美国的编辑希望我们这样做。

sentences.en: With LLMs, we can write in basic sentences and tell the LLM the idea and it converts that to nice writing.If you write each paragraph and have an LLM make that paragraph more scientific, it is entirely your paper, but it is flagged as LLM generated.

sentences.en: If you have an LLM write the paper but speak good enough English, you can make it look human even though it is not human.
sentences.zh: 如果你有法学硕士写论文，但英语说得足够好，你可以让它看起来很人性化，即使它不是人性的。

blocks.html_en:
It seems that LLMs often insert new ideas even when asked only to polish writing without changing it: https://bsky.app/profile/tomerullman.bsky.social/post/3mq33c...

I don't think this is surprising. Good technical writing is very precise. If you're starting from non-technical writing, I suspect that in most cases you can't make it "sound scientific" without adding new claims or changing the meaning. (Maybe you are being more careful, but this is something that worries me in general.)

sentences.en: (Maybe you are being more careful, but this is something that worries me in general.)
sentences.zh: （也许你更加小心了，但这总体上是让我担心的事情。）

blocks.html_en:
> If you write each paragraph and have an LLM make that paragraph more scientific, it is entirely your paper

"More scientific" is not some merely stylistic thing that faithfully preserves the original meaning of what you wrote. The precise details of each paragraph matters a lot in terms of what and how it communicates. The fact that these details do matter means that, according to my accounting, it is not entirely your paper.

Also, I find striving for "scientific" to be a pretty undesirable thing. Why should papers read like that? What is the benefit? The best papers (in terms of their writing and communication) are unpretentious and conversational. I'm pretty sure I'd prefer your "basic sentences", especially if they were wholly yours. (I understand that there are also external forces at play here as you mentioned.)

sentences.en: > If you write each paragraph and have an LLM make that paragraph more scientific, it is entirely your paper"More scientific" is not some merely stylistic thing that faithfully preserves the original meaning of what you wrote.
sentences.zh: > 如果你写了每个段落并让法学硕士使该段落更科学，那么这完全是你的论文“更科学”，而不仅仅是忠实保留你所写内容的原始含义的文体上的东西。

sentences.en: The precise details of each paragraph matters a lot in terms of what and how it communicates.
sentences.zh: 每个段落的精确细节对于其传达的内容和方式非常重要。

sentences.en: The fact that these details do matter means that, according to my accounting, it is not entirely your paper.Also, I find striving for "scientific" to be a pretty undesirable thing.
sentences.zh: 事实上，这些细节确实很重要，这意味着，根据我的统计，这并不完全是你的论文。而且，我发现追求“科学”是一件非常不可取的事情。

sentences.en: Why should papers read like that?
sentences.zh: 为什么论文应该这样读？

sentences.en: What is the benefit?
sentences.zh: 有什么好处？

sentences.en: The best papers (in terms of their writing and communication) are unpretentious and conversational.
sentences.zh: 最好的论文（就写作和交流而言）是朴实无华且对话式的。

sentences.en: I'm pretty sure I'd prefer your "basic sentences", especially if they were wholly yours.
sentences.zh: 我很确定我更喜欢你的“基本句子”，特别是如果它们完全是你的。

sentences.en: (I understand that there are also external forces at play here as you mentioned.)
sentences.zh: （我知道正如你提到的，这里也有外部力量在起作用。）

blocks.html_en: Being charitable, "more scientific" means more rigorous, reproducible, reliable based on the consensus of that scientific community (if any consensus exists, which it usually does in math and hard sciences), transparent in methodology, and candid about authorities cited and limitations to the experimental approach. Science is not a style guide; the purpose of a research publication is to impart a result to experts in the field and make claims that can be rigorously scrutinized. The best way to make a claim credibly is to demonstrate full fluency with the tools and techniques of the academic community in applying them thoughtfully to the chosen problem. But the result of doing that often looks downright impenetrable to the general public; see, e.g., Niven 1947 (1-page proof that pi is irrational). It's not the point of science to make it accessible to everyone. The point is to convince experts in the field of the correctness of the claim. The audiences are often totally different, and the tone and qualities of the writings likewise must vary.

sentences.en: Being charitable, "more scientific" means more rigorous, reproducible, reliable based on the consensus of that scientific community (if any consensus exists, which it usually does in math and hard sciences), transparent in methodology, and candid about authorities cited and limitations to the experimental approach.
sentences.zh: 出于慈善目的，“更科学”意味着基于科学界的共识（如果存在共识，通常在数学和硬科学中存在共识），更加严格、可重复、可靠，方法论透明，并且对所引用的权威和实验方法的局限性持坦率态度。

sentences.en: Science is not a style guide; the purpose of a research publication is to impart a result to experts in the field and make claims that can be rigorously scrutinized.
sentences.zh: 科学不是风格指南；而是风格指南。研究出版物的目的是向该领域的专家传达结果并提出可以严格审查的主张。

sentences.en: The best way to make a claim credibly is to demonstrate full fluency with the tools and techniques of the academic community in applying them thoughtfully to the chosen problem.
sentences.zh: 可信地提出主张的最佳方法是充分熟练地运用学术界的工具和技术，并将其深思熟虑地应用于所选问题。

sentences.en: But the result of doing that often looks downright impenetrable to the general public; see, e.g., Niven 1947 (1-page proof that pi is irrational).
sentences.zh: 但这样做的结果往往让公众觉得难以理解。例如，参见 Niven 1947（1 页证明 pi 是无理数）。

sentences.en: It's not the point of science to make it accessible to everyone.
sentences.zh: 科学的目的并不是让每个人都能接触到它。

sentences.en: The point is to convince experts in the field of the correctness of the claim.
sentences.zh: 关键是要让该领域的专家相信该主张的正确性。

sentences.en: The audiences are often totally different, and the tone and qualities of the writings likewise must vary.
sentences.zh: 读者往往完全不同，作品的基调和质量也必须有所不同。

blocks.html_en:
I've never had any trouble publishing in a more conversational style and have read plenty of papers that do the same. Of course, this varies by field and venue and format (journal vs. conference) and even reviewer and it's difficult to understand what the true constraints are and what the state of the publishing system is in this regard.

But I suspect that a lot of academic's feelings about it are informed by what others have told them and how they've been trained, rather than by what's actually permissible in the publishing system.

sentences.en: I've never had any trouble publishing in a more conversational style and have read plenty of papers that do the same.
sentences.zh: 我在以更具对话性的风格发表文章时从来没有遇到过任何麻烦，并且读过很多同样的论文。

sentences.en: Of course, this varies by field and venue and format (journal vs. conference) and even reviewer and it's difficult to understand what the true constraints are and what the state of the publishing system is in this regard.But I suspect that a lot of academic's feelings about it are informed by what others have told them and how they've been trained, rather than by what's actually permissible in the publishing system.
sentences.zh: 当然，这因领域、地点和格式（期刊与会议）甚至审稿人的不同而有所不同，很难理解真正的限制是什么以及出版系统在这方面的状况如何。但我怀疑很多学者对此的感受是由其他人告诉他们的以及他们如何接受培训决定的，而不是由出版系统实际允许的内容决定。

blocks.html_en:
My English is fine, and I did hand-write my MSc thesis in English, but scientific style writing is very tedious. Personally I don't think I'd even consider writing a paper "by hand" if I were to write another one.

There really is no point, as long as you verify the content matches your intent and edit out anything poorly written.

Frankly, I've read plenty of papers by native English-speakers over the years that'd strongly benefit from being rewritten by an LLM too...

sentences.en: My English is fine, and I did hand-write my MSc thesis in English, but scientific style writing is very tedious.
sentences.zh: 我的英语很好，我的硕士论文也是用英文手写的，但是科学风格的写作非常乏味。

sentences.en: Personally I don't think I'd even consider writing a paper "by hand" if I were to write another one.There really is no point, as long as you verify the content matches your intent and edit out anything poorly written.Frankly, I've read plenty of papers by native English-speakers over the years that'd strongly benefit from being rewritten by an LLM too...
sentences.zh: 就我个人而言，如果我要写另一篇论文，我什至不会考虑“手工”写一篇论文。实际上没有任何意义，只要你验证内容符合你的意图并删除任何写得不好的东西。坦率地说，这些年来我读过很多以英语为母语的人写的论文，这些论文也能从法学硕士重写中受益匪浅......

blocks.html_en:
> It is because we are bad at writing in a scientific style, and american editors expect us to do it. With LLMs, we can write in basic sentences and tell the LLM the idea and it converts that to nice writing.

This is a sin.

sentences.en: > It is because we are bad at writing in a scientific style, and american editors expect us to do it.

blocks.html_en: Which part is a sin? Using LLMs to deal with a lack of English-language fluency? I am a scientist (actually a mathematician, if it matters), and, if that's the way to deal with the practical hegemony of English in the scientific literature, then I have no problem with it. Rather that than people with important ideas can't get them before the scientific community. As long as the authors personally check and stand behind the scientific content of their papers, what do I care how the exposition was produced, especially if the role of LLMs is properly disclosed?

blocks.html_en:
I work with several researchers (primarily working on consensus protocols etc).

We were discussing research in general and I asked them: "Do you prefer the writing of the papers or the research?"

They, almost unanimously, agreed that they preferred the research. This makes sense as if they preferred writing they probably would have chosen another profession.

I say this b/c having LLMs available to turn research diagrams, code etc into a paper (or at least the starting point of a paper) will probably lead to MORE quality research papers. This is b/c I'm sure there was some friction in a researcher's mind of "I would love to do the research on this but don't want the trouble of writing the paper".

Put another way: on a 2D plot with one axis being the skills as a researcher and the other being hatred of writing, LLMs may "unlock" the people high on both axes to get more papers out.

Post Script: I agree that this could also lead to more BAD papers but the net may turn out to be positive in the long run.

sentences.en: I work with several researchers (primarily working on consensus protocols etc).We were discussing research in general and I asked them: "Do you prefer the writing of the papers or the research?"They, almost unanimously, agreed that they preferred the research.
sentences.zh: 我与几位研究人员一起工作（主要致力于共识协议等）。我们正在讨论一般性的研究，我问他们：“你更喜欢论文的写作还是研究？”他们几乎一致同意他们更喜欢研究。

sentences.en: This makes sense as if they preferred writing they probably would have chosen another profession.I say this b/c having LLMs available to turn research diagrams, code etc into a paper (or at least the starting point of a paper) will probably lead to MORE quality research papers.
sentences.zh: 这是有道理的，就好像他们更喜欢写作一样，他们可能会选择另一个职业。我说，拥有法学硕士可以将研究图表、代码等转化为论文（或至少是论文的起点），可能会产生更多高质量的研究论文。

sentences.en: This is b/c I'm sure there was some friction in a researcher's mind of "I would love to do the research on this but don't want the trouble of writing the paper".Put another way: on a 2D plot with one axis being the skills as a researcher and the other being hatred of writing, LLMs may "unlock" the people high on both axes to get more papers out.Post Script: I agree that this could also lead to more BAD papers but the net may turn out to be positive in the long run.
sentences.zh: 这是b/c，我确信研究人员的头脑中存在一些摩擦，“我很想对此进行研究，但不想写论文的麻烦”。换句话说：在一个二维图上，一个轴是作为研究人员的技能，另一个轴是对写作的仇恨，法学硕士可能会“解锁”两个轴上的人以获得更多论文。后记：我同意这也可能导致更多糟糕的论文，但网络可能会变成这样从长远来看要积极。

blocks.html_en:
One challenge with this approach is: could it be possible that the detector is simply learning to recognize words and jargon used more in the literature post 2022 as 'AI'?

For example, LLMs love to talk about LLMs (and the people who write with LLMs love to write about LLMs). Could "large language model" itself therefore be flagged as an AI-like phrase by this approach? It didn't exist much in the literature before 2022, does now, and certainly does more in AI-generated text: but, it is not actually a great way to distinguish modern AI generated text from human written text.

A helpful control would be to show that on some cohort of papers that can be declared reasonably clean of LLM generated text post 2023 there are very low rates compared to the arxiv.

For example, while papers in the journals Nature and Science are unlikely to be entirely LLM free at this point, if those were tested through 2026, we should see a line significantly lower than the arxiv's growth.

sentences.en: One challenge with this approach is: could it be possible that the detector is simply learning to recognize words and jargon used more in the literature post 2022 as 'AI'?For example, LLMs love to talk about LLMs (and the people who write with LLMs love to write about LLMs).
sentences.zh: 这种方法面临的一个挑战是：检测器是否有可能只是学习识别 2022 年后文献中更多使用的单词和术语，即“AI”？例如，法学硕士喜欢谈论法学硕士（法学硕士写作的人喜欢写有关法学硕士的文章）。

sentences.en: Could "large language model" itself therefore be flagged as an AI-like phrase by this approach?
sentences.zh: 因此，“大语言模型”本身是否可以通过这种方法被标记为类似人工智能的短语？

sentences.en: It didn't exist much in the literature before 2022, does now, and certainly does more in AI-generated text: but, it is not actually a great way to distinguish modern AI generated text from human written text.A helpful control would be to show that on some cohort of papers that can be declared reasonably clean of LLM generated text post 2023 there are very low rates compared to the arxiv.For example, while papers in the journals Nature and Science are unlikely to be entirely LLM free at this point, if those were tested through 2026, we should see a line significantly lower than the arxiv's growth.
sentences.zh: 在 2022 年之前，它在文献中并不存在，现在也存在，而且在人工智能生成的文本中肯定存在更多：但是，它实际上并不是区分现代人工智能生成的文本与人类书面文本的好方法。一个有用的控制是表明，在一些可以在 2023 年之后被宣布相当干净的 LLM 生成文本的论文中，与 arxiv 相比，比率非常低。例如，虽然《自然》和《科学》杂志上的论文目前不太可能完全不受 LLM 的影响，如果这些测试一直持续到 2026 年，我们应该会看到一条明显低于 arxiv 增长的线。

blocks.html_en:
One challenge with this approach is: could it be possible that the detector is simply learning to recognize words and jargon used more in the literature post 2022 as 'AI'?

For example, LLMs love to talk about LLMs (and the people who write with LLMs love to write about LLMs). Could "large language model" itself therefore be flagged as an AI-like phrase by this approach? It didn't exist much in the literature before 2022, does now, and certainly does more in AI-generated text: but, it is not actually a great way to distinguish modern AI generated text from human written text.

A helpful control would be to show that on some cohort of papers that can be declared reasonably clean of LLM generated text post 2023 there are very low rates compared to the arxiv.

For example, while papers in the journals Nature and Science are unlikely to be entirely LLM free at this point, if those were tested through 2026, we should see a line significantly lower than the arxiv's growth.

You raise a valid point. Just by intuition I'd say if this were true, it would probably just be a small fraction of the actual flagged articles. I will still look into how I can mitigate this when I update the detector.

The difficulty with this is then: How do you get a clean post 2023 dataset? I have no straightforward idea for this. You can't use other AI detectors to build it because then you'd never outperform them.

blocks.html_en:
I tried the detector on several of my own Stack Overflow answers, and the fourth or fifth one I tried was flagged as "85 % machine / likely machine-written" with the default settings, well above the default sensitivity threshold of 42%. When I turn off the option to strip LaTeX commands (the source is Markdown), that jumps to 96% machine.

Admittedly, a Stack Overflow answer is somewhat outside the realm of scientific writing, so it's still possible that the detector may be accurate within that domain. That said, it's a cautionary tale on the hazards of applying classifiers like this outside of the domain that they were trained on.

sentences.zh: 也就是说，这是一个关于在他们接受训练的领域之外应用此类分类器的危险的警示故事。

blocks.html_en:
Something I've thought about a lot is that there is having someone with some domain knowledge or reason to care a lot about a particular issue spend a bunch of tokens and cycles on it until something useful comes out the other end. The most obvious ones are the math problems that have been coming out and help push the frontier of various areas of math. Another example is taking all of the public NYC open data ecosystem and crunching it to get some value which I have spent a lot of time and tokens on but not found a great medium to share.

The question is just how to organize these outputs and conclusions in a way that is consistently reproducible and also how to correct errors or remove LLM nonsense where it refuses to take a position on something.

Before it made sense to do this in papers but it feels like we need something like a paper format.. that is fully reproducible ideally and optimized for aggregating knowledge in a better way. I.e. before a person spent months on one of these and there was just more filtering, and the output itself was a clear signal of time spent and effort that no longer exists.

sentences.en: Something I've thought about a lot is that there is having someone with some domain knowledge or reason to care a lot about a particular issue spend a bunch of tokens and cycles on it until something useful comes out the other end.
sentences.zh: 我思考过很多的事情是，有一些具有一定领域知识或有理由非常关心某个特定问题的人花费大量的代币和周期，直到另一端出现一些有用的东西。

sentences.en: The most obvious ones are the math problems that have been coming out and help push the frontier of various areas of math.
sentences.zh: 最明显的是已经出现的数学问题，它们有助于推动数学各个领域的前沿。

sentences.en: Another example is taking all of the public NYC open data ecosystem and crunching it to get some value which I have spent a lot of time and tokens on but not found a great medium to share.The question is just how to organize these outputs and conclusions in a way that is consistently reproducible and also how to correct errors or remove LLM nonsense where it refuses to take a position on something.Before it made sense to do this in papers but it feels like we need something like a paper format..
sentences.zh: 另一个例子是利用所有纽约市的公共开放数据生态系统并对其进行处理以获得一些价值，我花了很多时间和代币，但没有找到一个很好的媒介来分享。问题只是如何以一致可重复的方式组织这些输出和结论，以及如何纠正错误或删除LLM拒绝在某些事情上采取立场的废话。之前在论文中这样做是有意义的，但感觉我们需要类似纸张格式的东西。

sentences.en: that is fully reproducible ideally and optimized for aggregating knowledge in a better way.
sentences.zh: 理想情况下，这是完全可复制的，并且可以以更好的方式优化知识聚合。

sentences.en: I.e.
sentences.zh: IE。

sentences.en: before a person spent months on one of these and there was just more filtering, and the output itself was a clear signal of time spent and effort that no longer exists.
sentences.zh: 在此之前，人们花了几个月的时间在其中一个上，只是进行了更多的过滤，而输出本身就是一个明确的信号，表明所花费的时间和精力不再存在。

blocks.html_en:
Something I've thought about a lot is that there is having someone with some domain knowledge or reason to care a lot about a particular issue spend a bunch of tokens and cycles on it until something useful comes out the other end. The most obvious ones are the math problems that have been coming out and help push the frontier of various areas of math. Another example is taking all of the public NYC open data ecosystem and crunching it to get some value which I have spent a lot of time and tokens on but not found a great medium to share.

The question is just how to organize these outputs and conclusions in a way that is consistently reproducible and also how to correct errors or remove LLM nonsense where it refuses to take a position on something.

Before it made sense to do this in papers but it feels like we need something like a paper format.. that is fully reproducible ideally and optimized for aggregating knowledge in a better way. I.e. before a person spent months on one of these and there was just more filtering, and the output itself was a clear signal of time spent and effort that no longer exists.

blocks.html_en:
Yes, I’ve thought about this too. The strength of these models is that there is a lot more knowledge encoded in them than the average scientist has in mind at any given time. That means they can explore many more possible combinations of concepts.

If we imagine a set of all human ideas that these models have access to, then the set of possible discoveries would be something like the superset of all possible combinations of those ideas. I think all LLM discoveries are bounded by that space.

Looking at the recent OpenAI math discoveries, that seems to be pretty much what happened. Existing ideas were used as building blocks, the model found a valuable combination, and the result was something new that had real value.

blocks.html_en:
Yeah and I think even what might be the most useful is extracting how Codex got to a particular solution into a skill or into even more customized software so it can be applied in lots more places.

I know people are working on these things I just haven’t seen the right way yet. Like in manufacturing right now people are trying to encode what skilled machinists do into software and scale it up, we need to go further on that for Math/ data analysis etc.

blocks.html_en:
I get 0% (accurately) on my latest paper. Not super surprised, as I intentionally avoid some LLM-isms that I used to use because I don't want reviewers to have even the slightest indication that text is LLM-generated (even if in principle I'm not opposed to polishing or even wholesale generating academic text if it can convey the original research well, especially for non-native speakers).

I don't think the problem is as bad as a naive reading of this article suggests. I'm highly skeptical that anywhere near 65% of recent CS papers that I've read (mostly systems papers) are substantially AI-written. I threw some recent papers I've read into the system and they come back as 0-7%.

sentences.en: I get 0% (accurately) on my latest paper.
sentences.zh: 我最新论文的得分为 0%（准确）。

sentences.en: I'm not sure what to make of this.

sentences.en: Is this some kind of intentional irony?
sentences.zh: 这是某种故意的讽刺吗？

blocks.html_en:
To corroborate this - I found pretty similar rates of AI-flagged papers over time from running pangram on ArXiv (abstracts) in a physics subfield, which is currently at about 25% as of April (write up here: https://peterse.github.io/2026/06/15/The-rising-tide-pt1.htm...).

Its not clear from your writeup what threshold needs to be reached to be classified as "machine written". A preprint where half the text is human and half is 100% AI should be a different category than a preprint where 100% of the text is AI-assisted.

Also its cool that you're making the detector available. When you say "cheap to run", do you know how this compares to pricing for a commercial detector pangram or GPTZero?

sentences.en: To corroborate this - I found pretty similar rates of AI-flagged papers over time from running pangram on ArXiv (abstracts) in a physics subfield, which is currently at about 25% as of April (write up here: https://peterse.github.io/2026/06/15/The-rising-tide-pt1.htm...).Its not clear from your writeup what threshold needs to be reached to be classified as "machine written".
sentences.zh: 为了证实这一点 - 我发现随着时间的推移，在物理子领域的 ArXiv（摘要）上运行 pangram 时，人工智能标记论文的比率非常相似，截至 4 月份，该比例目前约为 25%（在这里写下：https://peterse.github.io/2026/06/15/The-rising-tide-pt1.htm...）。从你的文章中不清楚需要达到什么阈值才能被归类为“机器”写的”。

sentences.en: A preprint where half the text is human and half is 100% AI should be a different category than a preprint where 100% of the text is AI-assisted.Also its cool that you're making the detector available.
sentences.zh: 一半文本是人类文本、一半是 100% AI 的预印本应该与 100% 文本由 AI 辅助的预印本属于不同的类别。此外，您提供检测器也很酷。

sentences.en: When you say "cheap to run", do you know how this compares to pricing for a commercial detector pangram or GPTZero?
sentences.zh: 当您说“运行便宜”时，您知道这与商业探测器 pangram 或 GPTZero 的定价相比如何吗？

I’m very skeptical of these results. I run a pretty large group paper website on top of arXiv and we run pangram on papers. The numbers are not nearly this high.

One thing I see a lot is papers flagged as AI because they include llm rollouts in the paper as examples.

sentences.en: I’m very skeptical of these results.
sentences.zh: 我对这些结果非常怀疑。

the funniest part of these AI detectors is that if I were to upload any of einstin's paper's they will all be flagged as AI-written. it makes sense because it's part of their training data.

but this post makes me wonder, if more papers' are written with AI, or the shape of knowledge of converging?

blocks.html_en: People are saying this is a bad thing but is it really a problem? The compelling aspect of research is the data and/or description of work, not the writing. Papers probably should be written by AI so that they're clear and well presented, while the researchers should focus on generating good data. If there is no data or work behind the paper, we should question whether the research group needs funding.

blocks.html_en:
but the assumption you are making is that the underlying ideas are compelling. In practice, people often decide to publish incremental and/or mediocre work for various reasons, and then dress these ideas up to seem as compelling as possible to get past peer review or make a press release.

at the least, this is problematic for peer-review because the absolute number of submissions outpaces the time availability of a finite number of expert reviewers. We cannot quickly generate expert human reviewers, and so the community might converge towards half-baked solutions (AI-generated reviews or rejection systems, vastly expanded referee pools, etc.) that tend to erode trust and and make scientific communities more adversarial.

sentences.en: but the assumption you are making is that the underlying ideas are compelling.
sentences.zh: 但你所做的假设是基本想法是令人信服的。

sentences.en: In practice, people often decide to publish incremental and/or mediocre work for various reasons, and then dress these ideas up to seem as compelling as possible to get past peer review or make a press release.at the least, this is problematic for peer-review because the absolute number of submissions outpaces the time availability of a finite number of expert reviewers.
sentences.zh: 在实践中，人们经常出于各种原因决定发表增量和/或平庸的工作，然后将这些想法打扮得尽可能引人注目，以通过同行评审或发布新闻稿。至少，这对于同行评审来说是有问题的，因为提交的绝对数量超过了有限数量的专家评审员的可用时间。

sentences.en: We cannot quickly generate expert human reviewers, and so the community might converge towards half-baked solutions (AI-generated reviews or rejection systems, vastly expanded referee pools, etc.) that tend to erode trust and and make scientific communities more adversarial.
sentences.zh: 我们无法快速产生专业的人类审稿人，因此社区可能会趋向于不成熟的解决方案（人工智能生成的审稿或拒绝系统、大幅扩大的审稿人库等），这往往会削弱信任并使科学界更具敌对性。

blocks.html_en:
As an academic who is forced to review AI-generated conference/journal submissions, I can assure you this is not the case. LLM-heavy papers are terrible.

Even if they manage to avoid straight-up factual incorrectness, the writing is full of ambiguities and vagueness when you look closely. Meanwhile, space is wasted repeating the same claim in multiple ways, or explaining something simple.

They also seem unable to resist the hype/advertising tone, overselling the contribution while exaggerating the limitations of related work.

I would much rather read grammatically incorrect or awkward sentences.

While academic writing does have a few pointless historical conventions, the huge majority of "status quo writing style" is a logical consequence of 1) minimizing ambiguity, 2) organizing ideas coherently, 3) distinguishing opinion/interpretation from fact, and 4) providing enough detail to be reproducible.

sentences.en: As an academic who is forced to review AI-generated conference/journal submissions, I can assure you this is not the case.
sentences.zh: 作为一名被迫审查人工智能生成的会议/期刊提交的学者，我可以向你保证事实并非如此。

sentences.en: LLM-heavy papers are terrible.Even if they manage to avoid straight-up factual incorrectness, the writing is full of ambiguities and vagueness when you look closely.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士论文很糟糕。即使它们设法避免直接的事实错误，但当你仔细观察时，文章中的内容仍然充满了歧义和含糊之处。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, space is wasted repeating the same claim in multiple ways, or explaining something simple.They also seem unable to resist the hype/advertising tone, overselling the contribution while exaggerating the limitations of related work.I would much rather read grammatically incorrect or awkward sentences.While academic writing does have a few pointless historical conventions, the huge majority of "status quo writing style" is a logical consequence of 1) minimizing ambiguity, 2) organizing ideas coherently, 3) distinguishing opinion/interpretation from fact, and 4) providing enough detail to be reproducible.
sentences.zh: 同时，以多种方式重复相同的主张，或解释一些简单的东西，浪费了篇幅。他们似乎也无法抗拒炒作/广告的语气，在夸大相关工作的局限性的同时，夸大其贡献。我宁愿阅读语法不正确或尴尬的句子。虽然学术写作确实有一些毫无意义的历史惯例，但绝大多数“现状写作风格”是以下逻辑结果：1）最大限度地减少歧义，2）连贯地组织思想，3）区分来自事实的意见/解释，以及 4) 提供足够的可重复的细节。

blocks.html_en:
What's the point of looking when no one even cares?

consider the fraudster that went around suing people on the basis of his absurd claims of being bitcoin's creator. He's now transitioned to using AI to gather graduate degrees and is obtaining masters and doctoral degrees at a regular place and writing multiple 'papers' per day that are all quite obviously AI slop.

People report his cheating and publications and simply no one cares... and this is someone court adjudicated to have fabricated evidence in court on a massive scale, including through the use of AI.

But when it comes to the degrees and publication everyone involved that wanted paid got paid, and apparently that's all that matters.

sentences.en: What's the point of looking when no one even cares?consider the fraudster that went around suing people on the basis of his absurd claims of being bitcoin's creator.
sentences.zh: 当没有人关心的时候，寻找还有什么意义呢？想想那个骗子，他以自己是比特币创造者的荒谬说法为由四处起诉人们。

sentences.en: He's now transitioned to using AI to gather graduate degrees and is obtaining masters and doctoral degrees at a regular place and writing multiple 'papers' per day that are all quite obviously AI slop.People report his cheating and publications and simply no one cares...
sentences.zh: 他现在已经转向使用人工智能来获取研究生学位，并在固定的地方获得硕士和博士学位，每天写多篇“论文”，这些论文显然都是人工智能的废话。人们举报他的作弊和出版物，但根本没有人关心......

sentences.en: and this is someone court adjudicated to have fabricated evidence in court on a massive scale, including through the use of AI.But when it comes to the degrees and publication everyone involved that wanted paid got paid, and apparently that's all that matters.
sentences.zh: 这是一个被法院裁定在法庭上大规模伪造证据的人，包括通过使用人工智能。但当涉及到学位和出版物时，所有想要报酬的人都得到了报酬，显然这才是最重要的。

blocks.html_en:
The important question is:

So what?

Genuinely. I get that there may be some visceral reaction against this, but when I break it down, I mostly fail to see the problem. Seems like what is actually important is:

Compared to before, when a human reads it, do they -- or society -- get something good out of it? Is it worth it to add this to the "pantheon?"

If that's not what's happening enough, and if this doesn't describe the process -- then the problem lies elsewhere, no?

sentences.en: The important question is:So what?Genuinely.
sentences.zh: 重要的问题是：那又怎样？说实话。

sentences.zh: 我知道可能会有一些本能的反应，但当我分解它时，我大多看不到问题所在。

sentences.zh: 似乎真正重要的是：与以前相比，当人们阅读它时，他们——或者社会——能从中得到一些好处吗？

sentences.en: Is it worth it to add this to the "pantheon?"If that's not what's happening enough, and if this doesn't describe the process -- then the problem lies elsewhere, no?
sentences.zh: 是否值得将其添加到“万神殿”中？如果这还不够发生，并且如果这没有描述整个过程，那么问题就出在其他地方，不是吗？

blocks.html_en: If that's not what's happening enough, and if this doesn't describe the process -- then the problem lies elsewhere, no?

sentences.en: If that's not what's happening enough, and if this doesn't describe the process -- then the problem lies elsewhere, no?
sentences.zh: 如果这还不够发生，并且如果这没有描述整个过程——那么问题就出在其他地方，不是吗？

blocks.html_en:
I recently desk-rejected a paper where every single citation in its Introduction was hallucinated. That means that the entire connection between what the author(s) did and how it relates to existing research was simply made up. I've never seen this happening before AI but now there's at least one paper in every cycle pulling something similar.

My problem therefore is: we are seeing more and more papers written with tools that are known to make up facts, citations, and even entire papers. And the number of papers has increased, too. I therefore see it less as "people are being more productive" and more "people are releasing bad science much faster than we can keep up with".

sentences.en: I recently desk-rejected a paper where every single citation in its Introduction was hallucinated.
sentences.zh: 我最近拒绝了一篇论文，其中引言中的每一个引文都是幻觉。

sentences.en: That means that the entire connection between what the author(s) did and how it relates to existing research was simply made up.
sentences.zh: 这意味着作者所做的事情以及它与现有研究的关系之间的整个联系都是简单地虚构的。

sentences.en: I've never seen this happening before AI but now there's at least one paper in every cycle pulling something similar.My problem therefore is: we are seeing more and more papers written with tools that are known to make up facts, citations, and even entire papers.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能之前，我从未见过这种情况发生，但现在每个周期中至少有一篇论文提出了类似的东西。因此，我的问题是：我们看到越来越多的论文是用已知的工具撰写的，这些工具可以构成事实、引文，甚至整篇论文。

sentences.en: And the number of papers has increased, too.
sentences.zh: 而且论文数量也有所增加。

sentences.en: I therefore see it less as "people are being more productive" and more "people are releasing bad science much faster than we can keep up with".
sentences.zh: 因此，我认为它不再是“人们的生产力越来越高”，而是“人们发布糟糕的科学的速度比我们跟上的速度快得多”。

blocks.html_en: Yeah but that's an issue with the researcher putting out a bad paper, and it suggests you'll have to reject more papers. We wouldn't ban email because many of the emails are spam, it just means we need new tools to filter out junk. AI will allow researchers to be more productive all together and take less time to publish a paper, which is good.

sentences.en: Yeah but that's an issue with the researcher putting out a bad paper, and it suggests you'll have to reject more papers.
sentences.zh: 是的，但这就是研究人员发表糟糕论文的问题，这表明你将不得不拒绝更多论文。

sentences.en: We wouldn't ban email because many of the emails are spam, it just means we need new tools to filter out junk.
sentences.zh: 我们不会禁止电子邮件，因为许多电子邮件都是垃圾邮件，这只是意味着我们需要新的工具来过滤垃圾邮件。

sentences.en: AI will allow researchers to be more productive all together and take less time to publish a paper, which is good.
sentences.zh: 人工智能将使研究人员提高工作效率，并减少发表论文的时间，这是一件好事。

blocks.html_en:
The problem with spam is that it's not a technology like AI is. So I suggest taking cars instead.

Cars have plenty of advantages, and yet no one would say "the number of pedestrians killed by cars is rising, but that's an issue with the drivers". In fact, the opposite is true: from fines and school zones to speed bumps and bollards, we have accepted that cars bring structural problems with them that cannot be solved at the driver level alone.

> we need new tools to filter out junk

Agreed, but if my office suddenly was flooded with garbage my first thought wouldn't be "I need more, bigger trash cans" but rather "who brought all this junk here and why?". To simply assume that the garbage is a sudden natural phenomena that I have to live with seems, at the very least, unfair.

sentences.en: The problem with spam is that it's not a technology like AI is.
sentences.zh: 垃圾邮件的问题在于它不像人工智能那样是一种技术。

blocks.html_en:
> Yeah but that's an issue with the researcher putting out a bad paper, and it suggests you'll have to reject more papers. We wouldn't ban email because many of the emails are spam, it just means we need new tools to filter out junk. AI will allow researchers to be more productive all together and take less time to publish a paper, which is good.

It's a signal:noise ratio thing. If 1 out of every 1000 AI-written papers are bad, it makes sense to put in a filter that auto-rejects any paper that has AI tells.

After all, if that 1 researcher was any good, he wouldn't have used AI to write the thing in the first place.

Publishing was always about getting past the filters. There's one more filter - "AI-generated content" - so do what you have to to get past it. IOW, write your own paper.

sentences.en: > Yeah but that's an issue with the researcher putting out a bad paper, and it suggests you'll have to reject more papers.
sentences.zh: > 是的，但是研究人员发表了一篇糟糕的论文是一个问题，这表明你将不得不拒绝更多的论文。

sentences.en: We wouldn't ban email because many of the emails are spam, it just means we need new tools to filter out junk.
sentences.zh: 我们不会禁止电子邮件，因为许多电子邮件都是垃圾邮件，这只是意味着我们需要新的工具来过滤垃圾邮件。

sentences.en: AI will allow researchers to be more productive all together and take less time to publish a paper, which is good.It's a signal:noise ratio thing.
sentences.zh: 人工智能将使研究人员能够提高工作效率，并减少发表论文的时间，这是一件好事。这是一个信噪比的问题。

sentences.en: If 1 out of every 1000 AI-written papers are bad, it makes sense to put in a filter that auto-rejects any paper that has AI tells.After all, if that 1 researcher was any good, he wouldn't have used AI to write the thing in the first place.Publishing was always about getting past the filters.
sentences.zh: 如果每 1000 篇 AI 撰写的论文中就有 1 篇是糟糕的，那么设置一个过滤器来自动拒绝任何包含 AI 信息的论文是有意义的。毕竟，如果那位研究人员有任何优秀之处，他一开始就不会使用 AI 来写这篇文章。出版总是要超越过滤器。

sentences.en: There's one more filter - "AI-generated content" - so do what you have to to get past it.
sentences.zh: 还有一个过滤器——“人工智能生成的内容”——所以你必须采取措施才能通过它。

sentences.en: IOW, write your own paper.
sentences.zh: IOW，写你自己的论文。

blocks.html_en:
While working on my PhD, there was one guy in the field that would publish new, better results to Springer every time someone improved upon benchmarks. No code, no data, no conference publications, paywall restricted, unverifiable, always the best.

I am almost certain he was "hallucinating" the results. This was in the 2010s

There are well known issues in academic publishing, though I imagine it has become much noisier like open source

blocks.html_en:
Surprisingly I agree with you. My opinion is: if it makes communicating research more effective, while not reducing the quality of the output substantially, I see no issue.

A possible conclusion for this could be: If the majority of CS papers is AI written, let's just accept this reality universally and stop worrying about it altogether.

sentences.en: Surprisingly I agree with you.
sentences.zh: 令人惊讶的是我同意你的观点。

sentences.zh: 我的观点是：如果它使研究交流更加有效，同时又不会大幅降低输出的质量，我认为没有问题。对此的一个可能的结论可能是：如果大多数计算机科学论文都是人工智能撰写的，那么让我们普遍接受这一现实并完全停止担心它。

blocks.html_en:
I think the key question is more effective at what?

I see plenty of anecdotal evidence that models have been trained fantastically well—and getting better—at writing to trigger the right neurons in the human population to produce “This is interesting/informative/correct” responses in bulk.

Could their ability to produce those responses run far ahead of their ability to actually achieve the last in reality? Sure seems plausible, and then where are we?

sentences.en: I think the key question is more effective at what?I see plenty of anecdotal evidence that models have been trained fantastically well—and getting better—at writing to trigger the right neurons in the human population to produce “This is interesting/informative/correct” responses in bulk.Could their ability to produce those responses run far ahead of their ability to actually achieve the last in reality?

sentences.en: Sure seems plausible, and then where are we?
sentences.zh: 当然似乎有道理，那么我们在哪里呢？

blocks.html_en:
My hypothesis is that an LLM’s ability to concoct prose that will convince even an expert of the validity of an idea is largely independent of the LLM’s ability to validate the idea itself, or whether the idea is correct in the first place. Especially if it’s being prompted to do the former, not the latter.

My anecdotal evidence is the LLM-generated, inchoate technical dross that is routinely upvoted onto the hn front page. Much of it isn’t even coherent enough to be wrong, but the readership here finds it interesting!

sentences.zh: 其中大部分甚至不够连贯，不可能是错误的，但这里的读者发现它很有趣！

blocks.html_en:
> if it makes communicating research more effective, while not reducing the quality of the output substantially, I see no issue.

That's a big "If".

If a research is good, the author still has to clear all the hurdles in publishing. "Writing your own paper" is just one more hurdle.

> A possible conclusion for this could be: If the majority of CS papers is AI written, let's just accept this reality universally and stop worrying about it altogether.

That's just a different way of saying "if the majority of CS papers are crap, lets just accept this reality".

So, go on, publish away all your AI-induced "research", but the bar is slowly going to be raised anyway to reject that. That's how science always worked - when a bar is not sufficient to exclude the crap, it is raised.

blocks.html_en:
I reviewed for ACL (big NLP conf) and another similar conference recently. Lots of crap (though there always has been). One superficially well-written paper was likely AI-plagiarized (i.e. the AI sampled an idea from prior work and rewrote it) and got desk rejected for it.

Reviewers were also totally unengaged. Of 20 reviews I read (from my reviewers or from reviewers on the same papers), maybe 2 were mediocre, and the rest were crap (though likely not AI).

The notion that science will somehow benefit from this is about as stupid an idea as you can have. Science relies on skepticism. AIs are not skeptical, and many folks are submitting papers because they stand to gain something, not because they are motivated to do good research or develop new understanding. Fields are being inundated with garbage that is maximally indistinguishable from real work (that's the training objective for LLMs). This in turn maximizes the cost of identifying bad work.

This is the same enshittification process that we see everywhere else. You get spam phone calls because there is no reason for a spammer not to call you. "Researchers" are submitting spam papers because there is no cost to doing so with some possible gain. Absent intervention, this eventually drives the community value of the network to zero (or potentially negative, if friction costs to switching are high).

sentences.en: I reviewed for ACL (big NLP conf) and another similar conference recently.
sentences.zh: 我最近审阅了 ACL（big NLP conf）和另一个类似的会议。

blocks.html_en:
I reviewed for ACL (big NLP conf) and another similar conference recently. Lots of crap (though there always has been). One superficially well-written paper was likely AI-plagiarized (i.e. the AI sampled an idea from prior work and rewrote it) and got desk rejected for it.

Reviewers were also totally unengaged. Of 20 reviews I read (from my reviewers or from reviewers on the same papers), maybe 2 were mediocre, and the rest were crap (though likely not AI).

The notion that science will somehow benefit from this is about as stupid an idea as you can have. Science relies on skepticism. AIs are not skeptical, and many folks are submitting papers because they stand to gain something, not because they are motivated to do good research or develop new understanding. Fields are being inundated with garbage that is maximally indistinguishable from real work (that's the training objective for LLMs). This in turn maximizes the cost of identifying bad work.

This is the same enshittification process that we see everywhere else. You get spam phone calls because there is no reason for a spammer not to call you. "Researchers" are submitting spam papers because there is no cost to doing so with some possible gain. Absent intervention, this eventually drives the community value of the network to zero (or potentially negative, if friction costs to switching are high).

blocks.html_en:
> My opinion is: if it makes communicating research more effective, while not reducing the quality of the output substantially, I see no issue.

That's a big if. ArXiv is not peer reviewed and LLMs basically interpolate and extrapolate text, which makes them essentially fluff generators. Even in the most charitable interpretation, LLMs enable those with nothing to say to say nothing while meeting surface-level style guides.

sentences.en: > My opinion is: if it makes communicating research more effective, while not reducing the quality of the output substantially, I see no issue.That's a big if.
sentences.zh: > 我的观点是：如果它使研究交流更加有效，同时又不会大幅降低产出的质量，我认为没有问题。这是一个很大的假设。

sentences.en: ArXiv is not peer reviewed and LLMs basically interpolate and extrapolate text, which makes them essentially fluff generators.
sentences.zh: ArXiv 没有经过同行评审，法学硕士基本上是对文本进行插值和推断，这使得它们本质上是无用的生成器。

sentences.en: Even in the most charitable interpretation, LLMs enable those with nothing to say to say nothing while meeting surface-level style guides.
sentences.zh: 即使在最仁慈的解释中，法学硕士也能让那些无话可说的人在满足表面风格指南的同时无话可说。

blocks.html_en:
> (...) but I'd wager that the majority of these papers is not complete slop (...)

That's a huge assumption, and one that goes against the whole notion of using LLMs to generate text. AI slop is by far the norm.

blocks.html_en:
If a human writes a given paragraph, I know it has attained a minimum level of awareness in some human mind at some point. If an AI writes a paper, I have no guarantee that it has reached that minimum. We also fear, with some reason, a correlation between AI writing and possibly being hallucinated or non-existent. Not just because AI is bad and people should feel bad for using them, but because given that a paper is somehow fake, it is probably more likely to be made with AI, and running that backwards it is reasonable to take closer looks at AI papers.

If fake papers weren't already a big problem before AI and the fields had already been policing themselves adequately, if this was already a functioning high-trust domain, maybe we could ignore this a bit more, but the fields already manifestly had problems. People taking advantage of that are reasonably more likely to use AI. The pressures to publish or perish provide the voltage and the AIs are a rather convenient path-to-ground.

I agree in some sense that if a truth is published, it doesn't matter if the AI or a human published it. However there are perfectly reasonable reasons to be concerned that AI usage is correlated to not publishing truths, especially in a world where merely being human-generated was already not an adequate check against that.

sentences.en: If a human writes a given paragraph, I know it has attained a minimum level of awareness in some human mind at some point.
sentences.zh: 如果一个人写了一段给定的段落，我知道它在某些时候已经在某些人的头脑中达到了最低的意识水平。

sentences.en: If an AI writes a paper, I have no guarantee that it has reached that minimum.
sentences.zh: 如果人工智能写了一篇论文，我不能保证它已经达到了最低限度。

sentences.en: We also fear, with some reason, a correlation between AI writing and possibly being hallucinated or non-existent.
sentences.zh: 出于某种原因，我们还担心人工智能写作与可能产生幻觉或不存在之间存在关联。

sentences.en: Not just because AI is bad and people should feel bad for using them, but because given that a paper is somehow fake, it is probably more likely to be made with AI, and running that backwards it is reasonable to take closer looks at AI papers.If fake papers weren't already a big problem before AI and the fields had already been policing themselves adequately, if this was already a functioning high-trust domain, maybe we could ignore this a bit more, but the fields already manifestly had problems.
sentences.zh: 不仅因为人工智能不好，人们应该对使用它们感到难过，而且因为考虑到一篇论文在某种程度上是假的，它可能更有可能是用人工智能制作的，并且倒过来仔细观察人工智能论文是合理的。如果假论文在人工智能之前还不是一个大问题，并且各领域已经充分监管自己，如果这已经是一个正常运作的高信任领域，也许我们可以更多地忽略这一点，但这些领域显然已经存在问题了。

sentences.en: People taking advantage of that are reasonably more likely to use AI.
sentences.zh: 利用这一点的人们更有可能使用人工智能。

sentences.en: The pressures to publish or perish provide the voltage and the AIs are a rather convenient path-to-ground.I agree in some sense that if a truth is published, it doesn't matter if the AI or a human published it.
sentences.zh: 发表或灭亡的压力提供了电压，而人工智能是一条相当方便的接地路径。在某种意义上我同意，如果真理被发表，那么人工智能或人类发表它并不重要。

sentences.en: However there are perfectly reasonable reasons to be concerned that AI usage is correlated to not publishing truths, especially in a world where merely being human-generated was already not an adequate check against that.
sentences.zh: 然而，我们有完全合理的理由担心人工智能的使用与不发布真相有关，特别是在一个仅仅由人类生成已经不足以遏制这一点的世界中。

blocks.html_en:
Even if you can reject the aesthetic argument for non-fiction works (although read some of Dijkstra's papers for a good counterargument), it is still a problem because it breaks an important quality signalling mechanism.

Pre-LLMs, a paper with no spelling or grammar errors showed that somebody had put effort into writing and editing it. If they cared about the presentation, they probably also cared about the content. LLMs routinely produce nonsense that looks superficially like high-quality work.

There are far too many papers to read all of them. LLM slop is evidence that something is probably low quality. As the saying goes, "if you can't be bothered writing it, I can't be bothered reading." The rare outliers will get enough citations and recommendations to overcome this filter.

sentences.zh: 即使你可以拒绝非小说类作品的美学论证（尽管阅读迪杰斯特拉的一些论文以获得良好的反驳），它仍然是一个问题，因为它破坏了一个重要的质量信号机制。法学硕士预科，一篇没有拼写或语法错误的论文表明有人在撰写和编辑它时付出了努力。

blocks.html_en:
if 5% of the work that I'm reading to keep up is slop, that's a shame. that's time I spent puzzling over connections that weren't real, trying to impose some kind of logic on the arguments, and wondering why the data doesn't really support the conclusion.

if 50% of the work is nonsense, then there's a serious concern that we can't move forward at all.

blocks.html_en:
LLM-written text tends to have the property that it gives the impression of expertise and knowledge in excess of what the text actually contains.

In other words, it "sounds smart" without necessarily having anything to back it up. In even more critical terms, it's very good at bullshitting.

Unfortunately for us, the scientific community current relies on a certain amount of trust. (To do otherwise is very expensive! see: bitcoin). When you introduce a known-bullshitter to write your papers, every human in the loop effectively has to defend against an adversarial attack. Not just the readers at home, or the peer reviewers, but even the author needs to be wary that the facts and arguments coming out of the LLM are true and meaningful.

Personally, I've been a minor contributor to several high-profile papers. I don't know how every field does it, but in my experience, the corresponding author (generally the PI or other senior scientist), is responsible for the accuracy of the paper. They ultimately have to trust the people who did the work that the facts are true. Introducing LLMs into the mix make it more difficult for them to identify and review sections they are unsure of. (An honest person will typically write at a confidence level reflecting their certainty. LLMs do not do this in any reliable way.)

I've also found that LLMs frequently use metaphors that are unhelpful, or used out of context in a field that isn't familiar with them. This makes understanding the text more work, for no good reason. Introducing terms or definitions with low relevance reads as impressive at first glance, but avoiding the standard terminology in the field just adds confusion. (As an analogy, imagine if you were reading a CS paper that, for no particular reason, devoted a section to a new data structure called an "akimbo tree," which after much untangling, you realized was a reinvention of a randomized splay tree.)

sentences.en: LLM-written text tends to have the property that it gives the impression of expertise and knowledge in excess of what the text actually contains.In other words, it "sounds smart" without necessarily having anything to back it up.
sentences.zh: LLM 撰写的文本往往具有这样的特性，即它给人的印象是专业知识和知识超过了文本实际包含的内容。换句话说，它“听起来很聪明”，但不一定有任何支持。

sentences.en: In even more critical terms, it's very good at bullshitting.Unfortunately for us, the scientific community current relies on a certain amount of trust.
sentences.zh: 更关键的是，它非常擅长胡说八道。对我们来说不幸的是，当前的科学界依赖于一定程度的信任。

sentences.en: (To do otherwise is very expensive!

blocks.html_en: I've also found that LLMs frequently use metaphors that are unhelpful, or used out of context in a field that isn't familiar with them. This makes understanding the text more work, for no good reason. Introducing terms or definitions with low relevance reads as impressive at first glance, but avoiding the standard terminology in the field just adds confusion. (As an analogy, imagine if you were reading a CS paper that, for no particular reason, devoted a section to a new data structure called an "akimbo tree," which after much untangling, you realized was a reinvention of a randomized splay tree.)

sentences.en: I've also found that LLMs frequently use metaphors that are unhelpful, or used out of context in a field that isn't familiar with them.
sentences.zh: 我还发现法学硕士经常使用无用的隐喻，或者在不熟悉的领域中断章取义地使用隐喻。

sentences.en: This makes understanding the text more work, for no good reason.
sentences.zh: 这使得理解文本变得更加困难，但没有什么充分的理由。

sentences.en: Introducing terms or definitions with low relevance reads as impressive at first glance, but avoiding the standard terminology in the field just adds confusion.

sentences.en: (As an analogy, imagine if you were reading a CS paper that, for no particular reason, devoted a section to a new data structure called an "akimbo tree," which after much untangling, you realized was a reinvention of a randomized splay tree.)
sentences.zh: （打个比方，想象一下，如果您正在阅读一篇计算机科学论文，其中没有任何特殊原因，专门用一节介绍了一种称为“叉腰树”的新数据结构，经过多次理清后，您意识到这是对随机展开树的重新发明。）

blocks.html_en: Agreed. I know nothing about nuclear physics. I still doubt you could pick a random person off the street and have them convincingly pose as a nuclear physicist to explain a "nuclear physics" concept to me. I doubt you could do it with a random PhD from a non-physics field. An LLM could probably convince me even if 90% of the content of the explanation is subtly or blatantly incorrect.

sentences.en: Agreed.
sentences.zh: 同意。

sentences.zh: 我对核物理一无​​所知。

sentences.en: I doubt you could do it with a random PhD from a non-physics field.
sentences.zh: 我怀疑你可以用一个非物理学领域的随机博士学位来做到这一点。

sentences.zh: 即使 90% 的解释内容微妙或公然不正确，法学硕士也可能让我信服。

blocks.html_en: The problem is that humans start with credulity. AI hallucinates and makes up stuff some percentage of the time. Humans are not "default deny" when given information. Unleashing that was a disservice to mankind and has created a future filled with lies and people who are confident in them.

sentences.en: The problem is that humans start with credulity.
sentences.zh: 问题在于人类一开始就容易轻信。

sentences.en: AI hallucinates and makes up stuff some percentage of the time.
sentences.zh: 人工智能有时会产生幻觉并编造一些东西。

sentences.en: Humans are not "default deny" when given information.
sentences.zh: 当给予信息时，人类不会“默认拒绝”。

sentences.en: Unleashing that was a disservice to mankind and has created a future filled with lies and people who are confident in them.
sentences.zh: 释放这一点对人类来说是一种伤害，并创造了一个充满谎言和对谎言充满信心的人们的未来。

blocks.html_en:
LLMs are in essence an attack on the concept of written language, harvesting and dissolving the social contract that underpins it; it is no longer safe to assume that text has intent, let alone content, simply because it is grammatically well-formed.

Perhaps the most darkly amusing consequence of this particular mania is that by poisoning the majority of our information environment with hallucinated slop, we have likely crippled the next several generations of machine-learning techniques before they're even invented! Small, locally-hostable LLMs will rattle along spewing spam long after the broader "genai bubble" pops, and building clean training datasets will permanently be more difficult and expensive.

sentences.en: LLMs are in essence an attack on the concept of written language, harvesting and dissolving the social contract that underpins it; it is no longer safe to assume that text has intent, let alone content, simply because it is grammatically well-formed.Perhaps the most darkly amusing consequence of this particular mania is that by poisoning the majority of our information environment with hallucinated slop, we have likely crippled the next several generations of machine-learning techniques before they're even invented!
sentences.zh: 法学硕士本质上是对书面语言概念的攻击，收获并消解支撑它的社会契约；仅仅因为文本的语法结构良好，就不再安全地假设文本具有意图，更不用说内容了。也许这种特殊狂热最黑暗有趣的后果是，通过用幻觉的废话毒害我们的大多数信息环境，我们很可能在未来几代机器学习技术被发明之前就已经瘫痪了！

sentences.en: Small, locally-hostable LLMs will rattle along spewing spam long after the broader "genai bubble" pops, and building clean training datasets will permanently be more difficult and expensive.
sentences.zh: 在更广泛的“genai 泡沫”破裂很久之后，小型的、本地托管的法学硕士将继续不断地喷出垃圾邮件，而构建干净的训练数据集将永远变得更加困难和昂贵。

blocks.html_en:
Oh, to be technically correct:

AI hallucinates and makes up stuff 100% percent of the time. Never been a fan of that word for this.

Again, I fail to see the problem here that isn't solved by careful reading WHICH IS WHAT PEOPLE SHOULD BE DOING ANYWAY. I would like to see room for AI disclosure, maybe a statement of "this is how much AI I used."

But this blanket X% of this looks like AI? Again, so what?

sentences.zh: 哦，从技术上讲是正确的：人工智能 100% 的时间都会产生幻觉并编造东西。

sentences.en: Never been a fan of that word for this.Again, I fail to see the problem here that isn't solved by careful reading WHICH IS WHAT PEOPLE SHOULD BE DOING ANYWAY.

sentences.en: I would like to see room for AI disclosure, maybe a statement of "this is how much AI I used."But this blanket X% of this looks like AI?
sentences.zh: 我希望看到人工智能披露的空间，也许是“这是我使用了多少人工智能”的声明。但这其中的 X% 看起来像人工智能？

sentences.en: Again, so what?
sentences.zh: 再说一遍，那又怎样？

blocks.html_en:
If as a primary school student you had needed to reverify every fact /experiment that was presented in your textbooks, you would not have gotten very far.

Trust is very important to human progress.
